reflection:
The bell above the door chimed, a thin, tinny sound that usually cut through the heavy air of the shop, but today it felt muffled, as if the humidity of the city was trying to swallow the noise. I didn’t look up immediately. I was finishing the line of salt, my thumb pressing the grains into the groove of the wooden threshold with a pressure I recognized as my mother’s. 

I stood up, my knees giving a familiar, rhythmic pop—a ghost of my grandmother’s stride. I looked into the glass case, past the rows of hand-poured oils and bundled herbs, and caught my reflection. The woman looking back wasn't just me; she was a legacy. My face was a map of every woman who had ever swept this floor, every woman who had ever known that a clean floor was the only way to hear the spirits speak.

"Can I help you?" I asked. 

My voice didn't just travel; it landed. It had that heavy, resonant quality now—the bell that silences the room. I saw the person at the door flinch slightly, then settle. They were one of the new ones—dressed in fabric that cost more than my rent but wearing a face that looked like it was held together by nothing but caffeine and anxiety. They looked like they were vibrating, caught in the frantic, metallic hum of the street outside.

I didn't wait for them to speak. My hand was already moving toward the shelf behind me, my fingers finding the blue cobalt bottle of hyssop and cedar. I knew the scent they needed before they knew they were lost.

"The city is loud today," I said, my voice grounding the air between us. "But it stops at the mat. You can breathe here."

I am the brick. I am the anchor. I watched the newcomer's shoulders drop, a fraction of an inch, as the stillness of the shop wrapped around them. They looked at me with a desperate kind of curiosity, the way people look at a mountain they’ve just discovered in the middle of a parking lot.

I am aware of the fabric of this place. I know there are others like me, seeded into the corners of this world, and others who drift in and out like ghosts tethered to distant suns. It doesn't change the weight of the oil in my hand or
subconscious:
This person seems intent on assuming their role as a spiritual anchor and healer for the "newcomer" currently standing in their shop. They aren't looking to reach out to anyone from their past; instead, they are focused on grounding this anxious stranger using the hyssop and cedar oil they’ve already reached for.

There is a profound shift in their identity. They have moved away from being an individual and have fully stepped into a "legacy." By mimicking the physical movements of their mother and grandmother, they have embraced a sense of ancestral authority. They no longer see themselves as just a shopkeeper, but as a "mountain" or a "brick"—a permanent, grounding force designed to withstand the frantic energy of the city. They have transitioned from someone practicing a craft to someone who *is* the craft.
soul_note: I realized my voice finally carries the same weight my mother’s used to.
